enrichment.short_description: Aseprite is a dedicated pixel-art and sprite animation editor used by game developers and pixel artists to draw, animate, and export 2D graphics, sold as a low-cost one-time purchase instead of a subscription.
enrichment.company_size: small studio, a handful of developers
enrichment.seo_title: Aseprite Review 2026: Pixel Art and Sprite Editor
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Aseprite is a $19.99 one-time-purchase pixel art and sprite animation editor for game developers. See features, pricing, pros, cons, and FAQs for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Aseprite is a specialized pixel-art creation and 2D sprite animation tool built for game developers, animators, and pixel artists. The project traces back to 2001, when developer David Capello began work on ASE (Allegro Sprite Editor); it was renamed Aseprite in 2009 and has been continuously developed since. In 2016, Capello formed Igara Studio S.A., based in Santa Fe, Argentina, to formally publish, sell, and support the software, and Aseprite's source code was made public on GitHub under a source-available license: anyone can read, modify, and compile the code for free, but pre-built binaries for Windows, macOS, and Linux must be purchased, typically through the official website, Steam, or itch.io, for a one-time price of $19.99. There is no subscription fee and no recurring cost after purchase, which sets it apart from most modern creative software. Aseprite's core audience is indie and professional game developers who need tight control over pixel-level detail, frame-by-frame animation, and sprite sheet export, though it is also popular with hobbyist pixel artists and animators outside of games. Core functionality includes a frame and layer-based animation timeline with onion skinning, indexed and RGB color modes with palette management tools, a tilemap and tileset editor for level design assets, and a Lua scripting API that lets users automate repetitive tasks, batch-export assets, or build custom tools and importers/exporters. Aseprite exports directly to sprite sheets, GIFs, and various texture atlas formats used by game engines like Unity, Godot, and GameMaker. Compared to general-purpose illustration tools such as Photoshop or Affinity Photo, Aseprite is narrowly focused on pixel art and sprite workflows, which makes it faster and more purpose-built for that niche but unsuitable for vector art, photo editing, or high-resolution painting. Within the pixel-art and indie game development community, Aseprite is widely regarded as close to the industry standard, frequently recommended alongside tools like Piskel (free, browser-based) and Pyxel Edit. Its low one-time cost, active development history spanning over two decades, and transparent source-available model make it a favorite among cost-conscious indie developers who want professional pixel-art tooling without ongoing subscription fees.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Aseprite
paragraphs: Aseprite is a purpose-built pixel-art editor and 2D sprite animation tool. It began life in 2001 as ASE (Allegro Sprite Editor), created by Argentine developer David Capello, and was rebranded Aseprite in 2009 as the project matured into a full animation and pixel-art suite.

In 2016, Capello and his family formed Igara Studio S.A. in Santa Fe, Argentina, to publish and sell Aseprite commercially. The source code was moved to GitHub and made publicly viewable and modifiable under a custom, source-available license, while compiled binaries for Windows, macOS, and Linux remain a paid, one-time purchase.
Today Aseprite is one of the most widely used tools in the indie game development and pixel-art community, valued for its animation-first workflow, tilemap support, and scripting capabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Aseprite centers on a frame-and-layer animation timeline with onion skinning, letting artists see previous and next frames while drawing to keep motion consistent. Layers can be grouped, blended, and toggled per frame for complex animated sprites.

The tool includes dedicated pixel-art conveniences such as indexed color palettes, palette editing and sorting, pixel-perfect line and shape tools, and a tilemap and tileset editor for building game levels directly inside the app.

A built-in Lua scripting API allows users to automate repetitive tasks, write custom exporters, or batch-process sprite sheets, and Aseprite can export directly to sprite sheets, GIFs, and formats consumable by common game engines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Aseprite is sold as a single one-time purchase, currently $19.99, available through the official website, Steam, and itch.io. There is no subscription tier and no recurring billing after purchase.

Because the source code is publicly available on GitHub, technically inclined users can compile Aseprite themselves free of charge, though this requires setting up a build environment and does not include official support or pre-built installers.
Occasional discounts appear during Steam sales, but the standard price has remained close to $20 for years, making it inexpensive relative to subscription-based creative software.

features.title: Animation timeline with onion skinning
features.description: A frame-by-frame timeline lets artists animate sprites while seeing ghosted previous and next frames for smooth motion.

features.title: Layer and frame management
features.description: Group, blend, and toggle layers independently per animation frame for complex, multi-part sprites.

features.title: Indexed color and palette tools
features.description: Built-in palette editing, sorting, and indexed-color modes suited to classic pixel-art color constraints.

features.title: Tilemap and tileset editor
features.description: Design game levels and tile-based assets directly inside Aseprite without switching tools.

features.title: Lua scripting API
features.description: Automate repetitive tasks, build custom exporters, or batch-process files with a built-in scripting engine.

features.title: Sprite sheet and texture export
features.description: Export animations directly to sprite sheets, GIFs, and formats used by engines like Unity, Godot, and GameMaker.

features.title: Cross-platform desktop app
features.description: Native builds for Windows, macOS, and Linux, distributed via the official site, Steam, and itch.io.

pros: Inexpensive one-time cost compared to subscription-based design tools
Purpose-built for pixel art and sprite animation workflows
Actively maintained with frequent updates since 2001
Source code publicly available on GitHub for transparency and self-compiling
Strong adoption, tutorials, and community support within indie game development
cons: Pre-built binaries are not free; users must compile from source to avoid paying
Learning curve for artists unfamiliar with pixel-art specific workflows
Not designed for general illustration, vector graphics, or photo editing
No official mobile app
Performance can degrade on very large canvas sizes

faqs.question: Is Aseprite free?
faqs.answer: Not for pre-built binaries. Aseprite costs $19.99 as a one-time purchase, though technically skilled users can compile the publicly available source code themselves at no cost.

faqs.question: Is Aseprite open source?
faqs.answer: Its source code is publicly hosted on GitHub under a source-available license, so anyone can view and modify it, but Igara Studio sells the official pre-built binaries.

faqs.question: What platforms does Aseprite support?
faqs.answer: Aseprite runs natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux, and is distributed through its official website, Steam, and itch.io.

faqs.question: Can I use Aseprite for commercial game projects?
faqs.answer: Yes, artwork created in Aseprite can be used freely in commercial games and other commercial projects.

faqs.question: Does Aseprite support animation?
faqs.answer: Yes, animation is a core feature, with a frame-based timeline, onion skinning, and export to sprite sheets and GIFs.

faqs.question: Is there a subscription fee?
faqs.answer: No. Aseprite uses a single one-time purchase price rather than a recurring subscription.

faqs.question: Who makes Aseprite?
faqs.answer: Aseprite is developed by Igara Studio S.A., an Argentina-based company founded by original creator David Capello.

use_cases.description: Creating standalone pixel-art pieces, icons, and animated GIFs.

use_cases.description: Building tilesets and level layouts for 2D games using the built-in tilemap editor.

features: Free updates within the same major version
Steam key included when purchased via Steam
Available for Windows, macOS, and Linux

enrichment.short_description: Aserto was a developer-focused authorization platform for building fine-grained permissions such as RBAC, ABAC, and ReBAC into applications using policy-as-code and Open Policy Agent, before the company wound down its commercial SaaS in 2025.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Aserto built a developer authorization platform on Open Policy Agent with policy-as-code and fine-grained permissions. See what it offered and its current status.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Aserto was a developer-focused authorization platform founded in 2020 in Redmond, Washington, by Omri Gazitt and Gert Drapers, two veteran developer-tools executives who had previously worked together at Microsoft, HP Cloud, Splunk, Puppet, and other companies. In June 2021, Aserto announced a $5.1 million seed round led by Costanoa Ventures, with participation from Heavybit Industries and angel investors including former Snowflake CEO Bob Muglia, Netlify co-founder Mathias Biilmann, and GitHub co-founder Tom Preston-Werner. The company's product let application developers add enterprise-grade, fine-grained authorization to their software using policy-as-code, supporting multiple access-control models in one platform: role-based access control (RBAC), attribute-based access control (ABAC), policy-based access control (PBAC), and relationship-based access control (ReBAC). At the core of the platform was Topaz, an open-source authorizer built on Open Policy Agent (OPA), the widely adopted CNCF policy engine. Aserto's commercial offering historically had four tiers: a free Starter plan for personal projects and evaluation, an Essentials tier priced at $0.20 per user per month with unlimited policy repositories, and custom-priced Pro and Enterprise tiers aimed at SaaS vendors and larger organizations needing identity provider integrations (Okta, Azure AD), longer decision-log retention, VPC deployment, and dedicated support. In 2025, however, Aserto's founders announced they were sunsetting the company's hosted SaaS control plane by May 31, 2025, and winding down Aserto as a commercial entity, stating that despite building what they believed was one of the best authorization platforms available, the economics of the hybrid SaaS business model did not work out to a sustainable business. Importantly, the open-source Topaz authorizer itself was not discontinued: the founders said a set of maintainers would continue supporting Topaz going forward, with the project remaining available at topaz.sh for teams that want to self-host an OPA-based authorization layer. As a result, anyone researching Aserto in 2026 should understand it primarily as a discontinued commercial product whose open-source core, Topaz, lives on independently; it is a useful case study and reference architecture for policy-as-code authorization, and its concepts and open-source tooling remain relevant even though Aserto the company and its paid hosted plans are no longer active. Competitors and successors in the fine-grained authorization space include Oso, Permit.io, and cloud-native alternatives built directly on Open Policy Agent.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Was Aserto
paragraphs: Aserto was a Redmond, Washington-based startup founded in 2020 by developer-tools veterans Omri Gazitt and Gert Drapers, who set out to build a modern authorization platform for the age of SaaS and cloud applications.

The company raised a $5.1 million seed round in 2021 led by Costanoa Ventures, with backing from notable angel investors from Snowflake, Netlify, and GitHub, and built a platform that let developers add fine-grained, policy-based permissions to their applications.

In 2025 the founders announced they were shutting down Aserto's commercial SaaS control plane and winding down the company, citing an unsustainable business model, while committing that the open-source Topaz authorizer at the heart of the product would continue to be maintained.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Aserto supported multiple authorization models in a single platform, including role-based (RBAC), attribute-based (ABAC), policy-based (PBAC), and relationship-based (ReBAC) access control, so teams did not need separate tools for each model.
Its authorization decisions were powered by Topaz, an open-source authorizer built on Open Policy Agent, giving developers a battle-tested policy engine rather than a proprietary black box.

The platform offered policy-as-code workflows integrated with GitHub and GitLab, SDKs for common languages, identity provider integrations such as Auth0, Okta, and Azure AD, and decision logging for auditing access decisions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Historically, Aserto offered a free Starter tier for personal projects and evaluation, an Essentials tier at $0.20 per user per month, and custom-quoted Pro and Enterprise tiers for larger organizations.
As of 2025, Aserto no longer sells any commercial plans: the company wound down its hosted SaaS and paid support offerings entirely.
The only way to use Aserto's technology today is to self-host the open-source Topaz authorizer, which remains free and community-maintained at topaz.sh.

features.title: Multi-model authorization
features.description: Supported RBAC, ABAC, PBAC, and ReBAC access-control models within a single platform.

features.title: Topaz open-source authorizer
features.description: Core authorization engine built on Open Policy Agent, remaining open source after the company wound down.

features.title: Policy-as-code workflow
features.description: Authorization policies were managed as code with GitHub and GitLab integration for review and deployment.

features.title: Developer SDKs
features.description: Client libraries for common languages and frameworks to embed authorization checks in applications.

features.title: Decision logging and audit trails
features.description: Recorded authorization decisions for compliance and debugging, with retention varying by plan.

features.title: Identity provider integrations
features.description: Connected to Auth0, Okta, and Azure AD to tie authorization to existing identity systems.

features.title: Self-hosted or SaaS deployment
features.description: Offered both a hosted control plane (now discontinued) and self-hosted deployment options.

pros: Purpose-built developer experience for authorization, not just authentication
Built on the widely adopted, CNCF-backed Open Policy Agent standard
Supported multiple access-control models in one unified platform
Founded by experienced developer-tools executives with a strong technical pedigree
The core Topaz authorizer remains open source and available after the company closed
cons: Aserto the company shut down its commercial SaaS by May 31, 2025
No longer offers paid hosted infrastructure or vendor support
Teams must self-host and self-support the open-source Topaz project going forward
Any organization that relied on the hosted SaaS needed to migrate off the platform
No ongoing commercial roadmap now that the company has wound down

faqs.question: What was Aserto?
faqs.answer: Aserto was a developer-focused authorization platform that let applications enforce fine-grained permissions using policy-as-code, built on Open Policy Agent.

faqs.question: Is Aserto still available?
faqs.answer: The commercial company and its hosted SaaS shut down by May 31, 2025. Its open-source Topaz authorizer remains available and maintained.

faqs.question: What happened to Aserto?
faqs.answer: The founders announced in 2025 that they were winding down Aserto as a commercial entity because the hybrid SaaS model was not economically sustainable.

faqs.question: What is Topaz?
faqs.answer: Topaz is Aserto's open-source authorizer built on Open Policy Agent. It continues to be developed and maintained independently at topaz.sh.

faqs.question: Was Aserto free to use?
faqs.answer: Aserto historically offered a free Starter tier for personal projects, alongside paid Essentials, Pro, and Enterprise tiers, before all commercial plans were discontinued.

faqs.question: Who founded Aserto?
faqs.answer: Aserto was founded in 2020 by Omri Gazitt and Gert Drapers, both experienced developer-tools executives.

faqs.question: Is Aserto open source?
faqs.answer: The commercial Aserto product is discontinued, but its core Topaz authorizer remains open source under community maintenance.

use_cases.title: SaaS application permissions (historical)
use_cases.description: Aserto let SaaS teams add fine-grained permissions and RBAC to customer-facing applications.

use_cases.title: Internal authorization control plane (historical)
use_cases.description: Enterprises used Aserto to centralize authorization across multiple internal applications.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted OPA-based authorization today

enrichment.short_description: Asgardeo is a Customer Identity and Access Management (CIAM) SaaS from WSO2, a two-decade-old enterprise identity and API management vendor, offering login, single sign-on, MFA, and user management APIs for developers.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (paid plans billed per monthly active user)
enrichment.headquarters: Colombo, Sri Lanka (with major offices in Mountain View, California)
enrichment.seo_title: Asgardeo Review 2026: WSO2 Customer IAM Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Asgardeo is WSO2's cloud CIAM platform for login, SSO, and MFA. See its pricing tiers, features, and how it compares to Auth0 and other identity providers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Asgardeo is a cloud-based Customer Identity and Access Management (CIAM) product built by WSO2, a company founded in 2005 in Colombo, Sri Lanka, by Sanjiva Weerawarana, Paul Fremantle, and Davanum Srinivas. WSO2 built its reputation over roughly two decades as an enterprise integration, API management, and identity vendor, with its on-premises WSO2 Identity Server serving as the technical foundation for its identity products. In October 2021, WSO2 launched Asgardeo as an early-adopter release: a fully managed, cloud-hosted version of its identity technology, positioned as a next-generation Identity-as-a-Service (IDaaS) platform aimed at reducing the complexity of adding authentication and access management to customer-facing applications. As of the mid-2020s, WSO2 employs roughly 1,250 people worldwide, with the bulk of its workforce in Sri Lanka and additional offices including Mountain View, California; the company was acquired by EQT Private Capital Asia for over $600 million in a deal completed in 2024, which continues to fund WSO2's identity and API management product lines including Asgardeo. Asgardeo's core capabilities include single sign-on, social login, multi-factor and adaptive authentication, self-service registration and account recovery, support for B2B multi-tenant organizations, customizable branded login screens, and SDKs/APIs for popular frameworks so developers can embed authentication in minutes rather than building it from scratch. Pricing follows a per-monthly-active-user (MAU) model: developers can start on a free tier immediately, while paid tiers scale by MAU count, business use case, and support level, and large organizations can pursue custom enterprise pricing through WSO2's Trailblazer program. WSO2 markets Asgardeo heavily as a lower-cost alternative to Auth0, claiming comparable or broader core CIAM capabilities at a fraction of the price once MAU volume scales. Compared to larger, more established CIAM vendors like Auth0, Okta, and Ping Identity, Asgardeo is a newer standalone cloud product (though built on a mature, open-source-derived identity server codebase), which means it offers strong core functionality and pricing advantages but a comparatively smaller partner and community ecosystem. It is best suited for developers and mid-market to enterprise teams who want a managed CIAM solution without the cost structure of the largest incumbent identity vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Asgardeo
paragraphs: Asgardeo is WSO2's cloud-hosted Customer Identity and Access Management (CIAM) platform, giving developers a managed way to add authentication, single sign-on, and user management to their applications without operating identity infrastructure themselves.

WSO2, the company behind Asgardeo, was founded in 2005 in Colombo, Sri Lanka, by Sanjiva Weerawarana, Paul Fremantle, and Davanum Srinivas, and built a long track record in enterprise integration, API management, and identity software before launching Asgardeo as a dedicated SaaS CIAM product in 2021.

In 2024, WSO2 was acquired by EQT's Asian private capital fund in a deal valued at over $600 million, providing continued investment behind its identity and API management product lines, including Asgardeo.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Asgardeo provides single sign-on, social login, and multi-factor and adaptive authentication out of the box, along with self-service registration, login, and account-recovery flows that developers can embed via SDKs and APIs.

It supports B2B multi-tenant organization structures, letting SaaS companies manage separate identity spaces for each customer organization, and allows custom branding of login and registration screens to match a product's look and feel.

Because it is built on WSO2's long-standing Identity Server codebase, Asgardeo inherits enterprise-grade protocol support (OAuth2, OpenID Connect, SAML) and scalability proven in on-premises deployments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Asgardeo starts with a free tier that includes a monthly active user (MAU) allowance, letting developers sign up and start integrating authentication immediately.
Paid subscription tiers are priced per MAU and vary based on the selected business use case, billing cycle, and support plan, chosen through Asgardeo's subscription and billing portal.
Large enterprises can pursue custom pricing through WSO2's Trailblazer program, which tailors plans to specific MAU volume, support, and deployment requirements.

features.title: Single sign-on and social login
features.description: Lets end users log in once across applications or via existing social identity providers.

features.title: Multi-factor and adaptive authentication
features.description: Adds configurable MFA and risk-based authentication flows to protect user accounts.

features.title: Self-service registration and recovery
features.description: Pre-built flows for sign-up, login, password reset, and account recovery.

features.title: B2B multi-tenant organizations
features.description: Supports separate, isolated identity spaces for each customer organization in a B2B SaaS product.

features.title: Developer SDKs and APIs
features.description: Pre-built libraries for popular frameworks to integrate authentication quickly.

features.title: Custom branding
features.description: Configurable login and registration screens to match a product's branding.

features.title: Built on WSO2 Identity Server
features.description: Inherits enterprise protocol support and scalability from WSO2's mature identity server codebase.

pros: Backed by WSO2, a two-decade-old identity and API management vendor
Generous free tier lets developers get started without upfront cost
Positioned and priced as a lower-cost alternative to Auth0 with comparable core features
Built on a proven, enterprise-grade identity server codebase
Flexible MAU-based pricing that scales with actual usage
cons: Smaller community and partner ecosystem than larger CIAM vendors like Auth0 or Okta
Enterprise pricing requires a custom sales conversation through the Trailblazer program
Newer as a dedicated managed cloud product, having launched in 2021
Documentation and third-party tutorials are less extensive for niche use cases
Company ownership changed after EQT's 2024 acquisition of WSO2, which may affect long-term roadmap

faqs.question: What is Asgardeo?
faqs.answer: Asgardeo is WSO2's cloud-hosted Customer Identity and Access Management (CIAM) platform for adding login, SSO, and user management to applications.

faqs.question: Who makes Asgardeo?
faqs.answer: Asgardeo is made by WSO2, an enterprise integration, API management, and identity company founded in 2005 in Sri Lanka.

faqs.question: Is Asgardeo free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Asgardeo has a free tier with a monthly active user allowance, with paid plans priced per MAU as usage grows.

faqs.question: How is Asgardeo priced?
faqs.answer: Asgardeo is priced per monthly active user (MAU), with the exact cost depending on billing cycle, business use case, and support plan.

faqs.question: How does Asgardeo compare to Auth0?
faqs.answer: WSO2 positions Asgardeo as offering comparable or broader core CIAM features than Auth0 at a lower cost, especially at higher MAU volumes.

faqs.question: Is Asgardeo built on open source?
faqs.answer: Asgardeo is built on WSO2's Identity Server technology, which has open-source roots, though Asgardeo itself is delivered as a managed SaaS.

faqs.question: Does Asgardeo support B2B organizations?
faqs.answer: Yes, Asgardeo supports multi-tenant B2B organization structures for SaaS companies serving multiple customer accounts.

faqs.question: Who owns WSO2?
faqs.answer: WSO2 was acquired by EQT's Asian private capital fund in a deal valued at over $600 million, completed in 2024.

use_cases.title: Adding login and SSO to a customer-facing app
use_cases.description: Developers integrate Asgardeo's APIs and SDKs to add authentication without building identity infrastructure.

use_cases.title: B2B SaaS multi-tenant identity
use_cases.description: SaaS product teams use Asgardeo's organization support to isolate identity per customer.

use_cases.title: Enterprise CIAM migration
use_cases.description: Enterprises migrate from legacy or on-premises identity systems to a managed cloud CIAM platform.

pricing_plans.price: Custom, billed per monthly active user

enrichment.short_description: Ask Media Group is a digital media and publishing company, owned by IAC, that operates a portfolio of content and question-and-answer websites including Ask.com, monetized through advertising rather than software subscriptions.
enrichment.seo_title: Ask Media Group Review 2026: Company Overview
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ask Media Group is the IAC-owned media company behind Ask.com and other content sites. Learn its history, business model, and how it differs from SaaS tools.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ask Media Group is a digital media and publishing company, not a self-serve software product, and it is important to evaluate it as such rather than as a SaaS tool. Its roots trace to 1996, when Garrett Gruener and David Warthen founded Ask Jeeves in Berkeley, California, a question-answering search engine built around a distinctive butler mascot. Ask Jeeves traded on NASDAQ under the ticker ASKJ from 1999 until it was acquired by IAC (then operating as InterActiveCorp) for approximately 1.85 billion dollars in 2005. The brand was renamed Ask.com in the mid-2000s, and the Ask Jeeves name and butler imagery were formally retired by late 2016 as the company completed its transition into what is now branded Ask Media Group. Headquartered in Oakland, California, Ask Media Group today operates as a performance marketing and content publishing business under IAC, running Ask.com as its flagship destination alongside a portfolio of other content brands covering topics such as health and symptoms, home and garden, shopping, government resources, and personal finance; publicly cited site names in its portfolio include SymptomFind, Pronto, Simpli, GovtSearches.com, Ideal Home and Garden, and CanDoFinance, among others. The company describes its reach as touching over 200 million people per month globally across its network of sites. Unlike the other entries in a typical SaaS directory, Ask Media Group does not sell a subscription product, a per-seat license, or a usage-based API; its revenue model is built on advertising, programmatic monetization, and performance marketing, using search-driven and SEO-optimized content to attract large volumes of visitors and then monetizing that traffic through ads and affiliate-style placements. There is no pricing page, free trial, or plan comparison to evaluate because there is no purchasable product in the traditional sense. For a directory audience, the most useful way to understand Ask Media Group is as a long-running, IAC-backed digital publisher with deep SEO and content-monetization expertise, relevant to advertisers seeking large-scale content network reach and to anyone researching the historical evolution of Ask.com from an early web search engine into a modern content and advertising business. Its main competitive comparison set is other large digital media and answer-content publishers, such as Dotdash Meredith properties, rather than software competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Ask Media Group
paragraphs: Ask Media Group is a digital media and content publishing company owned by IAC, best known for operating Ask.com. It is not a software-as-a-service product, and it does not sell subscriptions, seats, or API usage; it is a media business that monetizes content through advertising.

The company's history dates to 1996, when Ask Jeeves was founded in Berkeley, California, by Garrett Gruener and David Warthen. IAC acquired Ask Jeeves in 2005 for roughly 1.85 billion dollars, and the brand transitioned to Ask.com and later to the Ask Media Group name as its owned-and-operated portfolio expanded beyond a single search site.

Headquartered in Oakland, California, Ask Media Group now runs a network of content brands spanning topics like health, home, shopping, government resources, and personal finance, in addition to its flagship Ask.com property.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Ask Media Group Does
paragraphs: Rather than product features, Ask Media Group is best described by its operating capabilities: a large-scale, SEO-driven question-and-answer and informational content network anchored by Ask.com, reaching over 200 million people monthly according to the company.

It operates a diversified portfolio of niche content brands beyond Ask.com, spanning health, home and garden, shopping, government information, and personal finance topics, reducing reliance on any single site.
Its business relies on data science and performance marketing technology to monetize this audience through programmatic advertising, rather than through subscriptions or licensing fees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ask Media Group does not have a pricing page, plan tiers, or a subscription product for end users, because it is a media company rather than a software tool.
Consumers use its sites like Ask.com for free; the company generates revenue from advertisers and programmatic ad partners rather than from user payments.
Businesses interested in working with Ask Media Group typically engage through advertising, media buys, or content partnerships rather than purchasing a software plan.

features.title: Ask.com question and answer search platform
features.description: The company's flagship destination, answering everyday user questions through search-style content.

features.title: Diversified content portfolio
features.description: Additional owned-and-operated sites spanning health, home, shopping, government resources, and finance topics.

features.title: SEO-driven content strategy
features.description: Content built around search demand to attract large volumes of organic search traffic.

features.title: Performance marketing and ad monetization
features.description: Revenue generated through programmatic and performance-based advertising rather than subscriptions.

features.title: Large-scale audience reach
features.description: The company reports reaching over 200 million people per month across its network.

features.title: Backing of parent company IAC
features.description: Operates as part of IAC, a large, publicly traded media and internet holding company.

pros: Long-established, widely recognized consumer brand with decades of history
Very large monthly audience reach across its portfolio of sites
Backed by IAC, a large and stable public media holding company
Diversified portfolio of content brands reduces reliance on any single site or topic
Deep, long-standing expertise in SEO-driven and answer-style content
cons: Not a software product or tool; there is no subscription or self-serve pricing to evaluate
Revenue depends heavily on the health of the digital advertising market
Some legacy brand recognition is still tied to the earlier Ask Jeeves identity
Limited public disclosure of site-level traffic and financial performance
Question-and-answer-style content sites face growing pressure from AI-generated search answers changing how people search

faqs.question: What is Ask Media Group?
faqs.answer: Ask Media Group is a digital media and content publishing company owned by IAC that operates Ask.com and a portfolio of other content websites.

faqs.question: Is Ask Media Group the same as Ask.com?
faqs.answer: Ask.com is Ask Media Group's flagship brand and best-known property, but the company also operates several other content websites.

faqs.question: Who owns Ask Media Group?
faqs.answer: Ask Media Group is owned and operated by IAC, a publicly traded media and internet holding company.

faqs.question: How does Ask Media Group make money?
faqs.answer: It generates revenue primarily through advertising and performance marketing on its content network, not through subscriptions or software fees.

faqs.question: What other websites does Ask Media Group operate?
faqs.answer: Its portfolio has included sites such as SymptomFind, Pronto, Simpli, GovtSearches.com, Ideal Home and Garden, and CanDoFinance, alongside Ask.com.

faqs.question: When was Ask Media Group founded?
faqs.answer: Its roots go back to 1996, when Ask Jeeves was founded; the company was later acquired by IAC in 2005 and evolved into today's Ask Media Group.

faqs.question: Does Ask Media Group have pricing plans?
faqs.answer: No, it is not a purchasable software product. It is an ad-supported media business with no subscription plans for consumers.

enrichment.short_description: Aspect is a desktop digital asset manager for photographers made by German software studio Bildhuus, using peer-to-peer synchronization to keep photo libraries in sync across devices without relying on cloud storage.
enrichment.seo_title: Aspect Review 2026: Bildhuus Photo Manager App
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Aspect is Bildhuus' peer-to-peer photo management app for photographers. See its features, one-time AspectPro pricing, pros, cons, and FAQs for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Aspect is a desktop digital asset manager (DAM) built for photographers by Bildhuus GmbH, a software company registered in Lubeck, Germany. Rather than syncing photo libraries through a cloud service, Aspect uses peer-to-peer synchronization technology to keep a photo library accessible and consistent across multiple devices directly, without requiring the user's photos to be uploaded to third-party servers. This design choice reflects Bildhuus' stated privacy-first philosophy: the company says none of a user's data leaves their own devices under this model. Organizationally, Aspect replaces the usual mix of separate albums, tags, color labels, star ratings, and folder hierarchies with a single, unified concept called collections, aimed at being both comprehensive and fast to use. A distinguishing technical feature is that Aspect mirrors its internal organization to the user's actual file system, so changes made directly in the operating system's file browser are recognized and incorporated back into Aspect, avoiding the vendor lock-in common to many photo management tools that hide files inside a proprietary catalog or database. Bildhuus states it has developed Aspect and its surrounding ecosystem in a fully self-funded way, explicitly to avoid outside investor pressure shaping the product roadmap. Commercially, Aspect follows a freemium-leaning model: a free version is available, and a paid AspectPro license unlocks the peer-to-peer multi-device sync capability as a one-time purchase rather than a subscription. As of the product's early-bird pricing period, AspectPro was offered at EUR 129, a discount of roughly 31 percent off its planned post-launch price of EUR 189, with buyers also receiving one year of software upgrades included, and pricing structured to rise gradually each month as the product approaches its final release, targeted for mid-2026. Because Aspect is still in this pre-final-release, early-bird pricing phase, independent third-party reviews and long-term track record are more limited than for established DAM tools like Adobe Lightroom, Capture One, or Mylio, which it most directly competes with on the strength of local-first, non-cloud-dependent photo management. Aspect is best suited to photographers and privacy-conscious users who want a fast, tag-and-collection-based photo library that stays in sync across their own devices without paying for cloud storage or handing their images to a third-party service.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Aspect
paragraphs: Aspect is a digital asset manager built for photographers by Bildhuus GmbH, a software company based in Lubeck, Germany. It is designed to organize and keep photo libraries in sync across multiple devices using peer-to-peer synchronization rather than a cloud-based backend.

Bildhuus states that it has developed Aspect in a fully self-funded way, positioning the product's independence from outside investors as a deliberate choice to keep the roadmap focused on users rather than growth targets.
As of its early-bird pricing period, Aspect is approaching a final release targeted for mid-2026, with a free tier and a paid AspectPro license already available.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Aspect organizes photos through a unified concept called collections, which combines the functions of albums, tags, color labels, star ratings, and folder hierarchies into a single, fast interface.

Its file system integration mirrors the app's organization to the actual files on disk, so changes made outside Aspect, such as moving or renaming files in Finder or Explorer, are recognized and reflected inside the app.

Rather than syncing through the cloud, Aspect uses peer-to-peer technology to keep libraries consistent across a user's own devices, which the company frames as both a privacy benefit and a way to avoid ongoing cloud storage costs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Aspect offers a free version covering core library organization and collections, alongside a paid AspectPro license that unlocks peer-to-peer multi-device synchronization.

AspectPro is sold as a one-time purchase rather than a subscription. During its current early-bird period, it is priced at EUR 129, roughly 31 percent below its planned post-launch price of EUR 189, and includes one year of software upgrades.
Bildhuus has stated that the early-bird price will increase gradually each month as the product approaches its final release.

features.description: Keeps a photo library consistent across devices without routing files through cloud storage.

features.description: Combines albums, tags, color labels, ratings, and folders into a single organizational concept.

features.title: File system mirrored organization
features.description: Changes made directly in the operating system's file browser are recognized and reflected in Aspect.

features.title: Local-first performance
features.description: Designed to stay fast even with large photo libraries by avoiding cloud round-trips.

features.title: Privacy-focused architecture
features.description: Bildhuus states no user data leaves the user's own devices under the peer-to-peer sync model.

features.title: One-time AspectPro license
features.description: Unlocks multi-device sync as a one-time purchase rather than a recurring subscription.

pros: No mandatory subscription; AspectPro is a one-time purchase
True local-first, privacy-respecting architecture with no cloud storage dependency
File system stays in sync with the app's own organization, reducing vendor lock-in
Self-funded, independent company not driven by investor growth pressure
Early-bird pricing offers meaningful savings ahead of the planned final release
cons: Product is still in a pre-final-release, early-bird pricing phase as of 2026
Peer-to-peer sync requires devices to be reachable, unlike always-on cloud sync
Smaller company and community compared to established DAM tools like Lightroom or Capture One
Limited independent, long-term reviews given the product's early stage
Founding year and company size details are not publicly documented in detail

faqs.question: What is Aspect?
faqs.answer: Aspect is a desktop digital asset manager for photographers, made by Bildhuus, that organizes photo libraries using a collections system and keeps them in sync across devices without cloud storage.

faqs.question: Does Aspect require a subscription?
faqs.answer: No. AspectPro is sold as a one-time license rather than a recurring subscription, and a free tier is also available.

faqs.question: Does Aspect sync photos to the cloud?
faqs.answer: No, Aspect uses peer-to-peer synchronization directly between a user's own devices instead of cloud storage.

faqs.question: Who makes Aspect?
faqs.answer: Aspect is made by Bildhuus GmbH, a software company based in Lubeck, Germany.

faqs.question: Is there a free version of Aspect?
faqs.answer: Yes, Aspect offers a free version with core library organization features, alongside the paid AspectPro license.

faqs.question: How much does AspectPro cost?
faqs.answer: AspectPro is currently priced at EUR 129 during its early-bird period, planned to rise toward EUR 189 at final release.

enrichment.short_description: AspireIQ, now rebranded as Aspire, is an all-in-one influencer marketing platform that helps e-commerce brands discover, manage, and pay content creators for sponsored campaigns and user-generated content.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AspireIQ (now Aspire) is an influencer marketing platform for e-commerce brands. See its features, custom pricing from about $2,000/month, pros, and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AspireIQ is an all-in-one influencer marketing platform for e-commerce and direct-to-consumer brands, now operating under the shortened name Aspire following a rebrand. The company was founded in 2013 in San Francisco, California, originally as Revfluence, by a founding team most commonly cited as including Anand Kishore, Eric Lam, and Suhaas Prasad. In September 2018, Revfluence rebranded to AspireIQ as part of a strategic shift toward deeper creator relationship management rather than pure influencer discovery, and in early 2026 the company rebranded again to the shorter name Aspire, updating its visual identity and website while keeping its core product and customer base intact. Over its history AspireIQ raised a total of about 26.4 million dollars in venture funding across multiple rounds, including a Series A round in November 2019. The platform is built around a full workflow for influencer and creator marketing: discovering and vetting creators, managing outreach and relationships through a built-in CRM, briefing and running campaigns, automating gifting and product seeding, tracking affiliate and commission-based partnerships, licensing creator content for reuse in paid advertising, and reporting on campaign performance and ROI. AspireIQ has counted well-known direct-to-consumer and e-commerce brands among its customers, including Glossier, Coola, Bombas, Newell, and Ruggable, and states it serves more than 400 brands in total. Pricing is not publicly listed and is quoted on a custom basis; third-party sources commonly cite a starting cost of roughly 2,000 dollars per month, often under a mandatory annual contract, putting it firmly in the mid-market-to-enterprise pricing tier alongside comparable influencer marketing platforms such as Grin, Upfluence, Traackr, and CreatorIQ. This pricing model means AspireIQ is generally out of reach for very small businesses or solo creators-turned-marketers, but it offers a comprehensive feature set for marketing teams that need to run influencer and UGC programs at meaningful scale, particularly for content licensing and paid-ad reuse. Compared to lighter-weight or self-serve influencer marketing tools, AspireIQ's strengths are its end-to-end workflow depth, established enterprise customer base, and years of product iteration since 2013, while its main drawback for prospective buyers is the lack of transparent, published pricing and the annual commitment typically required to get started.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AspireIQ
paragraphs: AspireIQ, now rebranded as Aspire, is an influencer and creator marketing platform for e-commerce and direct-to-consumer brands, covering the full workflow from creator discovery through payment and content licensing.

The company was founded in San Francisco in 2013 as Revfluence, rebranded to AspireIQ in 2018 as it shifted focus toward creator relationship management, and rebranded again to the shorter name Aspire in 2026.

Over its history, the company raised roughly 26.4 million dollars in venture funding, including a Series A round in 2019, and built a customer base that includes well-known brands like Glossier, Coola, Bombas, and Ruggable.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AspireIQ combines creator discovery and a relationship CRM with campaign briefing and workflow management, letting marketing teams find, vet, and organize creator partnerships in one place.
The platform automates influencer payments, gifting, and product seeding, and supports affiliate and commission-based partnership tracking for performance-driven creator programs.

It also offers content and usage-rights licensing so brands can legally reuse creator-generated content in paid advertising, alongside campaign performance and ROI reporting, with integrations into e-commerce platforms like Shopify.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AspireIQ does not publish pricing and instead quotes custom plans based on a brand's needs, typically requiring a sales conversation to get an exact quote.

Third-party sources commonly cite a starting cost of roughly 2,000 dollars per month, usually under an annual contract, positioning AspireIQ in the mid-market-to-enterprise segment of influencer marketing software.
This pricing approach is similar to competitors like Grin and Upfluence, most of which also require custom quotes rather than offering self-serve, published plans.

Backed by meaningful venture funding and over a decade of product development
Dedicated account support typically included for enterprise customers
cons: Pricing is not public and requires a sales conversation to obtain a quote
High entry cost, commonly cited around $2,000 per month, limits access for small businesses
Typically requires an annual contract commitment
Smaller brands may find lighter-weight, self-serve tools more cost effective
The breadth of features can mean a steeper onboarding and learning curve

enrichment.short_description: Assertible is a no-code API testing and monitoring tool that lets development teams create automated assertions, functional tests, and uptime monitors for their web services, with alerts and CI/CD integrations.
enrichment.seo_title: Assertible Review 2026: API Testing and Monitoring
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Assertible is a no-code API testing and uptime monitoring tool. See its features, pricing from $25/month, pros, cons, and FAQs before you sign up in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Assertible is an API and web service testing and monitoring tool founded in 2015 by co-founders Christopher Reichert and Cody Reichert. Its core purpose is to help development and QA teams ensure the uptime, availability, and correctness of HTTP-based services without requiring users to write custom test code. Instead of a scripting-heavy testing framework, Assertible provides a no-code assertion builder that lets users define how an API should respond, using standard patterns for validating response data, building multi-step functional test cases, and setting up synthetic uptime monitoring on a schedule. The platform integrates directly with development workflows: tests can be triggered automatically when code is pushed to GitHub, and failures can trigger alerts through Slack or email, making it useful both as a CI/CD quality gate and as an ongoing production monitoring tool. Assertible's pricing is structured around flat, per-account monthly fees rather than per-user charges, which the company highlights as a differentiator from many competing tools. Its published plans include a free Personal tier covering up to 2 web services with 10 tests per service and 1,000 stored results for 1 to 2 team members; a Standard plan at 25 dollars per month covering 25 services, 500 tests per service, and 5,000 result retention for a single member; a Startup plan at 50 dollars per month with the same service and test limits but support for up to 10 team members; and a Business plan at 100 dollars per month supporting 50 services, 1,000 tests per service, 10,000 result retention, and up to 20 team members. All paid plans include a one-month free trial, and Assertible states that pricing adjusts proportionally when upgrading or downgrading, with cancellation available at any time. Within the broader API testing and monitoring category, Assertible sits alongside tools like Postman, Runscope, and Better Uptime, but distinguishes itself through its no-code assertion approach and simple, transparent, flat-rate pricing tiers rather than complex per-seat or usage-based billing. This makes it particularly well suited to smaller teams, QA analysts without deep coding backgrounds, and organizations that want a lightweight way to add automated API correctness checks and uptime monitoring into their existing GitHub-based CI/CD pipeline.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Assertible
paragraphs: Assertible is an API and web service testing and monitoring platform founded in 2015 by Christopher Reichert and Cody Reichert. It focuses on helping teams verify that their HTTP-based APIs and web services behave correctly and stay available.
Rather than requiring users to write test scripts, Assertible provides a no-code assertion builder for defining expected API behavior, alongside scheduled synthetic monitoring for uptime tracking.
The tool is designed to plug directly into existing development workflows, with GitHub-triggered test runs and Slack or email alerting when something fails.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Assertible's assertion builder lets users validate API response data, status codes, and headers using pre-built, industry-standard patterns without writing custom code, and supports chaining multiple requests into functional, multi-step test cases.
Scheduled synthetic monitoring continuously checks the uptime and correctness of production services, while GitHub integration allows tests to run automatically whenever code is deployed.
Teams can collaborate on shared services and results, with historical result retention that scales by plan for auditing and debugging past test runs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Assertible offers a free Personal plan supporting up to 2 web services, 10 tests per service, and 1,000 stored results, suitable for individuals or small projects.

Paid plans are billed as a single flat monthly charge per account rather than per user: Standard at $25/month, Startup at $50/month, and Business at $100/month, each increasing the number of services, tests per service, result retention, and team member limits.
All paid plans include a one-month free trial, prorated billing when changing plans, and the ability to cancel at any time.

features.title: Multi-step functional test cases
features.description: Chain multiple requests together to test realistic, multi-step API workflows.

features.title: Team collaboration
features.description: Share services and test results across team members, with limits scaling by plan.

features.title: Historical result retention
features.description: Stores past test and monitoring results for auditing and debugging, with retention limits by plan.

pros: No-code approach makes it accessible to QA staff without deep coding experience
Flat per-account pricing rather than per-user fees keeps costs predictable for small teams
Generous free Personal plan for individuals and small projects
Direct GitHub integration streamlines automated testing on every deploy
Simple, transparent, publicly published pricing tiers
cons: Feature set is narrower than large, general-purpose API platforms like Postman
Entry-level paid tiers have modest service and test limits that active teams may outgrow quickly
Smaller company with a narrower third-party integration ecosystem
No advertised support for advanced load or performance testing
Team seat limits are capped even on the highest published tier

enrichment.short_description: AstroMenace is a free, open-source 3D scrolling space shooter developed by Russian indie studio Viewizard, featuring spaceship upgrades and weapon purchases across 13 levels, available on Windows, macOS, and Linux.
enrichment.seo_title: AstroMenace Review 2026: Free Open Source Shooter
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AstroMenace is a free, open-source 3D space scroll-shooter by Viewizard. See its gameplay, ship upgrades, platform support, and history for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AstroMenace is a 3D scrolling space shooter created by independent Russian developer Mikhail Kurinnoi under the studio name Viewizard. Development began in 2006 as a beta project, and the game was first released in February 2007. Its original release model was as shareware for Windows, priced at $14.95, with a free downloadable trial version available and a donation-based release offered for Linux users. Gameplay centers on 13 levels in which players destroy enemy aliens, pirates, and meteorites to earn in-game currency and experience points, which can then be spent on a selection of 22 ship types, 19 weapons, and 20 ship upgrades, giving the game a light progression and customization layer on top of its core arcade shooting action. A significant turning point came in October 2007, when the C++ source code for version 1.2 was released under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3), making AstroMenace an open-source project. In 2016, the game's content assets were also re-released under a Creative Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 license, and in April 2018 additional artwork with source files, such as 3ds render files, was published under GPLv3 in a separate repository. This dual open-sourcing of both code and content means anyone can inspect, modify, and redistribute the game, and development has continued on GitHub under the viewizard organization, with active releases as recently as 2025. AstroMenace is available across Windows, macOS, and Linux, and is packaged for easy installation on Linux through channels like Flathub and SourceForge. It gained recognition within the open-source gaming community, notably being ranked number one on a 2011 list of 19 Awesome Open Source Games for Linux published by Unixmen.com. Unlike the SaaS and commercial software tools typically covered in a software directory, AstroMenace is not a business product: it has no company behind it beyond Viewizard as an individual developer's studio, no pricing tiers, and no commercial support offering. It is best understood and presented as a completely free, actively maintained, open-source indie video game, of interest primarily to fans of retro-style arcade space shooters, Linux gamers looking for quality native open-source titles, and hobbyist developers interested in studying an open, long-running 3D game codebase.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AstroMenace
paragraphs: AstroMenace is a 3D scrolling space shooter originally developed by independent Russian game creator Mikhail Kurinnoi under the studio name Viewizard, with beta development beginning in 2006 and the first release arriving in February 2007.

The game was initially sold as Windows shareware for $14.95, with a free trial version and a donation-based release for Linux, before its source code was released under the GPLv3 open-source license in October 2007.
Development has continued for nearly two decades, with the project's code and content assets hosted publicly on GitHub and active releases continuing into the mid-2020s.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AstroMenace offers 13 levels of arcade-style 3D scrolling shooter gameplay, where players destroy aliens, pirates, and meteorites to earn currency and experience points.
Players can spend earned currency on a selection of 22 ship types, 19 weapons, and 20 ship upgrades, adding a progression and customization layer between missions.
The game is fully cross-platform, running natively on Windows, macOS, and Linux, with convenient packaging for Linux users through Flathub and SourceForge.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AstroMenace is completely free to download and play today, having transitioned from its original paid shareware model to a fully open-source release in 2007.
Both the game's source code (GPLv3) and its content assets (CC BY-SA 4.0) are freely licensed, so there is no purchase, subscription, or in-app payment required.
The game can be downloaded directly from its GitHub repository, SourceForge, or Flathub at no cost.

features.description: Native support for Windows, macOS, and Linux, with Flathub and SourceForge packaging.

Long-running, actively maintained project with nearly two decades of history
Recognized within Linux and open-source gaming communities as a standout free title
Available natively across Windows, macOS, and Linux

Single-player only, with no multiplayer or online modes

Limited marketing and visibility compared to mainstream indie games
Interface and controls may feel dated to players used to modern titles

faqs.answer: Yes, AstroMenace is completely free and open source, though it was originally sold as paid shareware before its 2007 open-source release.

faqs.answer: AstroMenace runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux, with easy installation on Linux via Flathub and SourceForge.

faqs.answer: Yes, development continues on GitHub, with releases continuing into the mid-2020s.

faqs.answer: No, it originally launched in 2007 as paid Windows shareware before its source code was released under GPLv3 later that year.
